content:
# Exodus Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~36r–37v (PDF pp. 69–70); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 49–50 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_009_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_009_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Fifth Plague: Livestock Disease (vv. 1–7)

**1** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh and tell him: This is what YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, says — Let my people go, so they may serve me.

**2** Because if you refuse to let them go and continue to hold them,

**3** then the hand of YHWH will strike your livestock in the field — the horses, the donkeys, the camels, the cattle, and the flocks — with a devastating plague.

**4** But YHWH will make a distinction between the livestock of Israel and the livestock of Egypt, and nothing belonging to the Israelites will die."

**5** YHWH set a time, saying, "Tomorrow YHWH will do this in the land."

**6** And YHWH did it the next day. All the livestock of Egypt died, but not a single animal belonging to the Israelites died.

**7** Pharaoh investigated and discovered that not even one of the Israelite animals had died. Yet Pharaoh's heart remained heavy, and he did not let the people go.

### The Sixth Plague: Boils (vv. 8–12)

**8** Then YHWH said to Moses and Aaron, "Take handfuls of soot from a kiln, and let Moses throw it into the air in Pharaoh's sight.

**9** It will become fine dust over the entire land of Egypt and will cause festering boils to break out on people and animals throughout the land of Egypt."

**10** So they took soot from a kiln and stood before Pharaoh. Moses threw it into the air, and it caused festering boils to break out on people and animals.

**11** The magicians could not even stand before Moses because of the boils — the boils were on the magicians just as on all the other Egyptians.

**12** But YHWH made Pharaoh's heart unyielding, and he did not listen to them — just as YHWH had spoken to Moses.

### The Seventh Plague: Hail (vv. 13–35)

**13** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Get up early in the morning, station yourself before Pharaoh, and say to him: This is what YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, says — Let my people go, so they may serve me.

**14** Because this time I am about to send the full force of my plagues against your heart, against your officials, and against your people — so that you will know there is no one like me in all the earth.

**15** By now I could have stretched out my hand and struck you and your people with a plague that would have wiped you off the earth.

**16** But this is precisely why I have raised you up — to display my power through you, and so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.

**17** You are still setting yourself up against my people by refusing to let them go.

**18** So at this time tomorrow I will rain down the most devastating hail that has ever fallen on Egypt from the day it was founded until now.

**19** Now then — send word and bring your livestock and everything you have in the field to safety. Every person and animal left in the open field that is not brought under shelter will die when the hail comes down on them."

**20** Those among Pharaoh's officials who took the word of YHWH seriously rushed to bring their slaves and livestock under shelter.

**21** But those who paid no attention to the word of YHWH left their slaves and livestock in the field.

**22** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the sky so that hail may fall on the entire land of Egypt — on people, on animals, and on every plant in the field throughout Egypt."

**23** Moses stretched out his staff toward the sky, and YHWH sent thunder and hail, and lightning struck the ground. YHWH rained hail on the land of Egypt.

**24** There was hail, and fire flashing continuously within the hail — so severe that nothing like it had ever struck the land of Egypt since it became a nation.

**25** The hail struck everything in the open field throughout the land of Egypt — people, animals, every plant in the field. The hail shattered every tree in the field.

**26** Only in the land of Goshen, where the Israelites lived, was there no hail.

**27** Pharaoh sent for Moses and Aaron and said to them, "This time I have sinned. YHWH is the righteous one, and I and my people are the guilty ones.

**28** Plead with YHWH — there has been more than enough of God's thunder and hail. I will let you go; you do not have to stay any longer."

**29** Moses said to him, "When I have gone out of the city, I will spread my hands out to YHWH. The thunder will stop, and there will be no more hail — so that you will know that the earth belongs to YHWH.

**30** But as for you and your officials — I know that you do not yet truly fear YHWH God."

**31** (The flax and the barley were destroyed, because the barley was in the ear and the flax was in bloom.

**32** But the wheat and the spelt were not destroyed, because they ripen later.)

**33** Moses left Pharaoh, went out of the city, and spread his hands out to YHWH. The thunder and hail stopped, and the rain no longer poured down on the land.

**34** When Pharaoh saw that the rain, the hail, and the thunder had stopped, he sinned again: he made his heart heavy — he and his officials.

**35** Pharaoh's heart became unyielding, and he did not let the Israelites go — just as YHWH had spoken through Moses.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "YHWH, the God of the Hebrews" (Heb. *YHWH Elohei ha'Ivrim*, יְהוָ֞ה אֱלֹהֵ֤י הָעִבְרִים֙):** Beginning with the fifth plague, God's self-identification expands. He is not merely "YHWH" but "YHWH, the God of the Hebrews." This title will recur at the opening of the seventh plague (v.13) and the eighth (10:3). The addition situates YHWH within a specific relationship — he is not an abstract deity but the God of a particular people whom Pharaoh is oppressing. The word *Ivrim* (עברים, "Hebrews") is the outsider-designation, the name Egyptians would use. God meets Pharaoh in Pharaoh's own categories: "the God of those slaves you hold."

**v.3 — "the hand of YHWH" (Heb. *yad-YHWH*, יַד־יְהוָ֜ה):** This is the first explicit mention of YHWH's *hand* as the instrument of plague. Previously we heard of the "finger of God" from the magicians (8:19). The escalation from finger to hand signifies intensification — what was once a mere finger's work now requires the full force of God's hand. The listed livestock — horses (*sus*, סוּס), donkeys (*chamor*, חמור), camels (*gamal*, גמל), cattle (*baqar*, בקר), and flocks (*tso'n*, צאן) — represents the entirety of Egypt's animal wealth and economic infrastructure. This plague targets not bodies (as gnats and swarms did) but the economy. The Vaticanus uses *cheir kuriou* (χεὶρ κυρίου), "hand of the Lord."

**v.4 — "YHWH will make a distinction" (Heb. *vehiflah YHWH*, וְהִפְלָ֣ה יְהוָ֔ה):** The Goshen distinction established in 8:22 now extends to livestock. The verb *hiflah* (from *palah*, פלה) returns — the same root used in 8:22 (*vehifleiti*). God's distinguishing act is becoming a structural feature of the plagues rather than a one-time event. The distinction is now explicitly between Israelite *property* and Egyptian *property*, expanding the scope of divine protection from persons to possessions. This anticipates the Passover's ultimate distinction between Israelite firstborn and Egyptian firstborn.

**v.6 — "All the livestock of Egypt died" (Heb. *vayyamot kol-miqneh Mitsrayim*, וַיָּ֕מָת כֹּ֖ל מִקְנֵ֣ה מִצְרָ֑יִם):** The word *kol* ("all") here has generated much discussion, since Egyptian livestock appear again in the hail plague (vv.19-21) and in the firstborn plague (12:29). The Hebrew *kol* can function as a strong generalizer ("all the livestock") or a categorical one ("every kind of livestock"). The narrative logic suggests massive but not literally total destruction — the livestock "in the field" (v.3) were targeted, while those in shelters may have survived. Alternatively, the interval between plagues allowed for restocking through trade. PNEUMA translates literally ("all") and lets the narrative context handle the scope, as the Hebrew text does.

**v.7 — "Pharaoh investigated" (Heb. *vayyishlach Par'oh*, וַיִּשְׁלַ֣ח פַּרְעֹ֔ה):** The verb *shalach* here means "sent" — Pharaoh sent messengers to verify whether Israelite livestock had survived. This detail is devastating. Pharaoh now *knows* the distinction is real. He has empirical proof that YHWH's word is precise, that the God of the Hebrews can discriminate between peoples at will. And yet: "Pharaoh's heart remained heavy" (*vayyikhbad lev Par'oh*, וַיִּכְבַּ֖ד לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֑ה). Here the *kaved*-hardening is in the Qal stem — the heart simply *was heavy*, presented as a state rather than a deliberate act. Even confirmed evidence does not move him. The Vaticanus uses *ebarunthē hē kardia Pharaō* (ἐβαρύνθη ἡ καρδία Φαραώ), "Pharaoh's heart was made heavy," passive voice.

**vv.8-10 — The kiln soot (Heb. *piach kivshan*, פִּ֣יחַ כִּבְשָׁ֔ן):** The sixth plague, like the third, comes without warning — no demand, no ultimatum. This confirms the three-cycle structure: plagues 1-2-3, 4-5-6, and 7-8-9, with each third plague in the cycle arriving unannounced. The material is *piach* (פיח), "soot, ashes," taken from a *kivshan* (כבשן), a brick kiln or smelting furnace. The symbolism is potent: the kilns of Egypt — the very furnaces where Israelite slaves burned bricks — now become the source of Egypt's torment. The instrument of oppression becomes the instrument of judgment. Moses throws the soot heavenward (*hashamaymah*, הַשָּׁמָ֑יְמָה), and it descends as *avaq* (אבק), "fine dust," covering the land. What goes up as the residue of slavery comes down as plague. The Vaticanus preserves the kiln imagery: *aithalēn kaminiaian* (αἰθάλην καμιναίαν), "soot from a furnace."

**v.10 — "festering boils" (Heb. *shechin avabu'ot pore'ach*, שְׁחִ֥ין אֲבַעְבֻּעֹ֖ת פֹּרֵ֑חַ):** The Hebrew piles three words for maximum visceral effect: *shechin* (שחין, "boils, inflammatory skin disease"), *avabu'ot* (אבעבעות, "blisters, pustules"), and *pore'ach* (פרח, "breaking out, erupting, blossoming"). The irony of the last word is bitter — *parach* normally describes flowers blooming. The boils "blossom" on human skin. This plague is the first to directly attack the human body, crossing a threshold from environmental devastation (water, animals, insects) to physical suffering. The Vaticanus uses *helkē phlusides* (ἕλκη φλυκτίδες), "sores with blisters."

**v.11 — The magicians' final humiliation:** The magicians, who failed to replicate the gnats (8:18), now cannot even *stand before Moses* (*lo-yakhelu hachartummim la'amod lifnei Mosheh*, לֹא־יָכְל֣וּ הַחַרְטֻמִּ֗ים לַעֲמֹד֙ לִפְנֵ֣י מֹשֶׁ֔ה). The progression of their degradation is complete: they matched the first two plagues, failed at the third, confessed divine power at the third, and now are themselves victims of the sixth. They bear the boils "like all the Egyptians" (*ki-hayah hashechin bachartummim uvekhol-Mitsrayim*) — they are no longer a class apart, no longer the experts. They are just more suffering Egyptians. Their disappearance from the narrative after this verse is permanent. The magical establishment of Egypt has been systematically dismantled across six plagues: replication, replication, failure, confession, absence, affliction. The Vaticanus notes their inability to stand: *ouk ēdunanto hoi pharmakoi stēnai* (οὐκ ἠδύναντο οἱ φαρμακοὶ στῆναι).

**v.12 — THE THEOLOGICAL TURNING POINT: YHWH hardens Pharaoh's heart (Heb. *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh*, וַיְחַזֵּ֤ק יְהוָה֙ אֶת־לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֔ה):** This verse is one of the most consequential in the entire Torah. For the first time in the narrative, YHWH is the *direct agent* of the *chazaq*-hardening. The verb is Piel — intensive, active, causative: YHWH *made rigid* Pharaoh's heart. Up through five plagues, every hardening has been either Pharaoh's own doing (8:15, 8:32 — Hiphil *kaved*, Pharaoh as subject) or described as a state (7:13, 7:22, 8:19 — Qal *chazaq*, the heart simply "being/becoming rigid"). YHWH announced the hardening programmatically in 4:21 and 7:3, but until this moment, he had not enacted it. Now he does.

The theological weight of this transition is enormous. The narrative has carefully established that Pharaoh hardened himself first — five plagues of self-inflicted resistance — before YHWH ratified and intensified that trajectory. This is not arbitrary divine manipulation of an innocent man. It is the confirmation of a direction Pharaoh himself chose. God gives Pharaoh over to what Pharaoh already wanted. The Piel stem (*yechazzeq*, יְחַזֵּק) implies not merely "allowing" rigidity but actively *strengthening* it — locking it in, making it permanent. From this point forward, YHWH's direct hardening will become the dominant pattern. The door Pharaoh chose to walk through is now locked behind him.

The phrase *ka'asher dibber YHWH el-Mosheh* (כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר דִּבֶּ֥ר יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶֽׁה), "just as YHWH had spoken to Moses," ties this back to the programmatic announcement: what God said would happen has now happened. Prophecy and fulfillment converge. The Vaticanus uses *esklērunen kurios* (ἐσκλήρυνεν κύριος), "the Lord hardened" — notably using *sklērunō* (σκληρύνω, "to make hard/stubborn"), the same verb Paul will invoke in Romans 9:18 (*hon thelei sklērunei*, "whom he wills he hardens") when he takes up this very passage to discuss divine sovereignty and human responsibility.

**v.14 — "the full force of my plagues" (Heb. *kol-maggefotai*, כָּל־מַגֵּפֹתַ֗י):** YHWH's speech beginning at v.13 is the longest divine speech in the plague narrative so far. It marks a categorical shift: from this point forward, the plagues are accompanied by theological exposition. God no longer merely acts — he explains. The phrase "all my plagues" (*kol-maggefotai*) is directed *el-libbeka* (אֶל־לִבֶּ֙ךָ֙), "against your heart." The plagues are aimed not just at Egypt's land, livestock, and bodies but at Pharaoh's inner self — the very heart that has been progressively hardened. The purpose is recognition: "so that you will know there is no one like me in all the earth" (*ki ein kamoni bekhol-ha'arets*, כִּ֛י אֵ֥ין כָּמֹ֖נִי בְּכָל־הָאָֽרֶץ). The claim of incomparability has expanded from "in this land" (8:22) to "in all the earth." The scope of YHWH's self-revelation is growing.

**vv.15-16 — "I have raised you up" (Heb. *he'emadtikha*, הֶעֱמַדְתִּ֑יךָ):** These two verses constitute one of the most theologically dense statements in the Hebrew Bible. Verse 15 is a counterfactual: "I could have struck you... and you would have been wiped off the earth" (*vatikkached min-ha'arets*, וַתִּכָּחֵ֖ד מִן־הָאָֽרֶץ). The verb *kachad* (כחד, Niphal) means "to be annihilated, to be hidden from existence." God could have ended this at any time. He chose not to.

Verse 16 gives the reason: *ve'ulam ba'avur zot he'emadtikha* — "But for this very reason I have raised you up." The verb *he'emadtikha* (from *amad*, עמד, Hiphil) literally means "I have caused you to stand" — that is, "I have established you, sustained you in your position, kept you alive and in power." This is not about Pharaoh's birth or his ascent to the throne but about his ongoing existence in the face of divine judgment. God is saying: the reason you are still here, still Pharaoh, still breathing, is because I have a purpose for you. That purpose is twofold: *ba'avur har'otka et-kochi* (בַּעֲב֖וּר הַרְאֹתְךָ֣ אֶת־כֹּחִ֑י), "to show you my power," and *ulema'an sapper shemi bekhol-ha'arets* (וּלְמַ֛עַן סַפֵּ֥ר שְׁמִ֖י בְּכָל־הָאָֽרֶץ), "so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth."

Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:17 (from the LXX): *eis auto touto exēgeira se* (εἰς αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἐξήγειρά σε), "for this very purpose I raised you up." The Vaticanus here reads *eneken toutou dietērēthēs* (ἕνεκεν τούτου διετηρήθης), "for this reason you were preserved" — a slightly different nuance, emphasizing preservation rather than raising. Paul's citation draws on a textual tradition that uses *exegeirō* ("to raise up, to bring on the stage of history"), which strengthens the idea that Pharaoh's entire existence serves a purpose within God's larger plan. The theological implication is staggering: Pharaoh, the most powerful human being on earth, is a character in YHWH's story — not the other way around. His resistance does not thwart God's purpose; it *serves* God's purpose. His defiance becomes the canvas on which divine power is displayed for the nations. This is the deepest statement of divine sovereignty in the plague narrative.

**v.17 — "setting yourself up against" (Heb. *mistolel*, מִסְתּוֹלֵ֖ל):** The verb *mistolel* (from *salal*, סלל, Hithpolel) is rare and debated. It may derive from *salal*, "to heap up, to build a road/ramp," yielding the sense "to exalt oneself, to set up a barricade." PNEUMA renders it as "setting yourself up against" to capture the self-exalting and obstructing force. The Vaticanus uses *empoiē* (ἐμποιῇ), from a root suggesting "to act within/against." The image is of Pharaoh piling himself up like a siege ramp against God's people — and, implicitly, against God himself.

**vv.19-21 — The warning and the division within Egypt:** For the first time, YHWH offers a way of escape *within Egypt*. People who take God's word seriously can shelter their livestock and servants. This creates a remarkable division — not between Israel and Egypt, but *within Egypt itself*. The response splits Pharaoh's own officials: "those who feared the word of YHWH" (*hayare et-devar YHWH*, הַיָּרֵ֖א אֶת־דְּבַ֣ר יְהוָ֑ה) brought their people to shelter, while "those who did not set their heart on the word of YHWH" (*asher lo-sam libbo el-devar YHWH*, אֲשֶׁ֤ר לֹא־שָׂם֙ לִבּ֔וֹ אֶל־דְּבַ֣ר יְהוָ֔ה) left them in the field.

The language here is extraordinary. The verb *yare* (ירא, "to fear") anticipates v.30, where Moses will accuse Pharaoh and his officials of *not yet fearing* YHWH. Some Egyptians are already more responsive to YHWH's word than Pharaoh is. The phrase *sam libbo* (שם לבו, "set his heart") is a deliberate echo of the hardening theme — the question throughout the plague narrative is what Pharaoh *does with his heart*. Here, Egyptian officials who "set their heart on YHWH's word" are saved; those who do not, perish. This is the exodus narrative's clearest anticipation of a "mixed multitude" (12:38) — non-Israelites who respond to YHWH's word.

**v.23 — "thunder and hail, and lightning struck the ground" (Heb. *qolot uvarad vattihalakh esh artsah*, קֹלֹ֣ת וּבָרָ֔ד וַתִּ֥הַלַּךְ אֵ֖שׁ אָ֑רְצָה):** The word *qolot* (קולות) means both "thunder" and "voices" — the heavens are speaking. The fire *walks* on the ground (*vattihalakh*, from *halak*, הלך) — not merely "struck" but moved along the surface, alive and directional. The Vaticanus describes the scene with similar intensity: *phōnai kai chalaza, kai dietrechen to pur epi tēs gēs* (φωναὶ καὶ χάλαζα, καὶ διέτρεχεν τὸ πῦρ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς), "voices and hail, and fire ran along upon the earth." This is theophanic language — the same elements (thunder, fire, hail) that will appear at Sinai (19:16-18). The plague is a preview of Sinai's terrifying glory, but here it falls as judgment rather than revelation. Or rather: it is revelation *through* judgment.

**v.24 — "fire flashing continuously within the hail" (Heb. *ve'esh mitlaqqachat betokh habbarad*, וְאֵ֕שׁ מִתְלַקַּ֖חַת בְּת֣וֹךְ הַבָּרָ֑ד):** The image is of fire *inside* ice — a physical impossibility that signals cosmic disruption. The Hithpael of *laqach* suggests fire "taking hold of itself," self-sustaining, self-feeding. The phrase *kaved me'od* (כָבֵ֣ד מְאֹ֔ד, "very heavy/severe") uses the root *kaved* — the same root as the hardening verb. The plague is "heavy" (*kaved*) just as Pharaoh's heart is "heavy" (*kaved*). The weight of judgment mirrors the weight of resistance. The narrator adds: *asher lo-hayah kamouhu bekhol-erets Mitsrayim me'az hayetah legoy* — "nothing like it had ever struck Egypt since it became a nation." The plagues are now exceeding all historical precedent.

**v.27 — Pharaoh's first confession of guilt (Heb. *chatati happa'am YHWH hatsaddiq va'ani ve'ammi haresh'aim*, חָטָ֖אתִי הַפָּ֑עַם יְהוָה֙ הַצַּדִּ֔יק וַאֲנִ֥י וְעַמִּ֖י הָרְשָׁעִֽים):** This is the most extraordinary speech Pharaoh has uttered. Three elements demand attention:

First, *chatati* (חטאתי, "I have sinned") — Pharaoh uses the standard confession formula. The word *happa'am* (הפעם, "this time") may suggest "this time [I admit it]" or "this time [I have gone too far]." Either way, it is the first explicit acknowledgment of personal guilt.

Second, *YHWH hatsaddiq* (יהוה הצדיק, "YHWH is the righteous one") — Pharaoh declares YHWH *tsaddiq*, a legal-covenantal term meaning "in the right, just, righteous." This is covenant language emerging from a pagan king's mouth. He is conceding the legal case: YHWH's claim on Israel is just; his own resistance is unjust.

Third, *va'ani ve'ammi haresh'aim* (ואני ועמי הרשעים, "and I and my people are the guilty ones") — the antonym pair *tsaddiq/rasha* (righteous/wicked) is the fundamental binary of Israelite legal and wisdom literature. Pharaoh assigns himself and his people the *rasha* position. This is a complete judicial confession.

And yet Moses sees through it (v.30). The confession is produced by terror, not transformation. It is the language of a man who wants the hail to stop, not a man who has genuinely recognized YHWH's sovereignty. The Vaticanus renders the confession faithfully: *hēmartēka... kurios dikaios* (ἡμάρτηκα... κύριος δίκαιος). The gap between Pharaoh's words and his heart is precisely what the hardening theme is designed to expose.

**v.29 — "the earth belongs to YHWH" (Heb. *ki la-YHWH ha'arets*, כִּ֥י לַיהוָ֖ה הָאָֽרֶץ):** Moses names the theological principle at stake. The stopping of the hail will demonstrate not Pharaoh's repentance but YHWH's ownership. The earth — *ha'arets* — is YHWH's. Egypt is YHWH's. The Nile is YHWH's. The livestock are YHWH's. Pharaoh's palace is YHWH's. The entire exodus is a property dispute, and YHWH is asserting title over everything Pharaoh claims to possess. This phrase echoes Psalm 24:1 (*la-YHWH ha'arets umelo'ah*, "the earth is YHWH's and its fullness") and establishes a creation-theology foundation for the plagues: YHWH can command the elements because they belong to him.

**v.30 — Moses sees through the repentance (Heb. *terem tir'un mippenei YHWH Elohim*, טֶ֣רֶם תִּֽירְא֔וּן מִפְּנֵ֖י יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהִֽים):** Moses' response to Pharaoh's confession is devastating: "I know that you and your officials do not yet fear YHWH God." The word *terem* (טרם, "not yet") is important — it does not say "you will never fear" but "you do not *yet* fear." There is a faint opening: the future remains undetermined. But the present is clear. Moses can distinguish between fear of hail and fear of God — between wanting the consequences to stop and genuinely submitting to the one who sends them.

The full divine title *YHWH Elohim* (יהוה אלהים) is rare in Exodus and recalls the creation narrative (Gen. 2-3), where it is the dominant name for God. Its use here, in a passage about cosmic forces and the earth belonging to YHWH, reinforces the creation-theology of the plague narrative. The Vaticanus preserves the full title: *kurion ton theon* (κύριον τὸν θεόν).

**vv.31-32 — The agricultural parenthesis:** The narrator pauses to note which crops were destroyed (flax and barley — early-season crops) and which survived (wheat and spelt — *ki afilot hennah*, כִּ֥י אֲפִילֹ֖ת הֵֽנָּה, "because they are late-ripening"). The word *afilot* (אפילות) means "late, dark, hidden" — the later crops had not yet emerged and were protected by the ground. This botanical precision serves the narrative: it explains why the locust plague (ch. 10) will still find crops to destroy. The detail also tells us the season: flax blooms and barley forms heads in late January to February in Egypt. The plagues are taking place in late winter, pointing toward the spring Passover.

**v.34 — "he sinned again: he made his heart heavy" (Heb. *vayyosef lachato vayyakhbed libbo*, וַיּ֣וֹסֶף לַחֲטֹ֔א וַיַּכְבֵּ֥ד לִבּ֖וֹ):** The narrator's verdict is withering. The verb *yasaf* (יסף, "to add, to continue") means Pharaoh *added* sinning — he piled it on. And the specific sin is *kaved*-hardening: he made his heart heavy. After his own confession that YHWH is righteous and he is guilty, Pharaoh reverts. The gap between v.27 ("I have sinned... YHWH is righteous") and v.34 ("he sinned again and made his heart heavy") exposes the mechanism of the hardening cycle at its clearest: correct theology without genuine submission produces only deeper guilt. Pharaoh can say the right words — he can even mean them in the moment of terror — but the moment the pressure lifts, he returns to his default. The narrator adds *hu va'avadav* (ה֥וּא וַעֲבָדָֽיו), "he and his officials," spreading the blame. The entire apparatus of Egyptian power is complicit.

**v.35 — "Pharaoh's heart became unyielding" (Heb. *vayyechezaq lev Par'oh*, וַיֶּחֱזַ֤ק לֵב־פַּרְעֹה֙):** The chapter ends with *chazaq*-hardening — the heart becomes rigid. After the Piel divine hardening of v.12, this Qal form in v.35 could be read as the ongoing effect of that divine action. The formula *ka'asher dibber YHWH beyad-Mosheh* (כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר דִּבֶּ֥ר יְהוָ֖ה בְּיַד־מֹשֶֽׁה), "just as YHWH had spoken *through* Moses" (literally "by the hand of Moses"), uses *beyad* rather than the earlier *el* ("to"). YHWH now speaks *through* Moses as an instrument, not merely *to* Moses as a recipient. Moses' prophetic authority has deepened.

---

**The structural pattern of Exodus 9 — three plagues completing the second and beginning the third cycle:**

| Plague | Cycle Position | Warning | Target | Hardening |
|--------|---------------|---------|--------|-----------|
| 5 — Livestock disease (vv.1-7) | Cycle 2, plague 1 | Warning at Pharaoh | Economy/livestock | *Kaved* Qal — heart "was heavy" (state) |
| 6 — Boils (vv.8-12) | Cycle 2, plague 3 | No warning | Human bodies | *Chazaq* Piel — **YHWH hardens** (turning point) |
| 7 — Hail (vv.13-35) | Cycle 3, plague 1 | Warning (extended theological speech) | Crops/field/bodies | *Kaved* Hiphil + *Chazaq* Qal (double) |

The escalation within this chapter is dramatic: the fifth plague kills livestock, the sixth afflicts bodies, and the seventh combines lethal hail, fire, and thunder — approaching cosmic warfare. Simultaneously, the divine speeches grow longer and more theologically explicit. By v.16, God is explaining *why Pharaoh exists*. The plagues are no longer just punishments — they are a classroom, and the lesson is about who rules the earth.

**Hardening verb tracker for Exodus 9:**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 9:7 | *kaved* (כבד) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "remained heavy" |
| 9:12 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (first direct divine hardening) | "made unyielding" |
| 9:34 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 9:35 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state/effect) | "became unyielding" |

**Cumulative tracker (Exodus 7–9):**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 7:3 | *qashah* (קשה) | Hiphil | YHWH (announced) | "harden" |
| 7:13 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 7:14 | *kaved* (כבד) | Adjective | Description of state | "unyielding" |
| 7:22 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:15 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 8:19 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:32 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| **9:7** | *kaved* (כבד) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "remained heavy" |
| **9:12** | ***chazaq*** **(חזק)** | **Piel** | **YHWH (direct)** | **"made unyielding"** |
| **9:34** | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| **9:35** | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "became unyielding" |

The pattern is now irrevocable. Through plagues 1-5, Pharaoh was the sole agent of his own hardening. At plague 6, YHWH intervened directly for the first time. At plague 7, the pattern doubles — Pharaoh hardens himself *and* the state of rigidity persists. The self-hardening and the divine hardening are converging into a single, irreversible trajectory. Pharaoh's confession in v.27 ("YHWH is righteous") makes the tragedy complete: he *knows* the truth and cannot act on it. The heart that could once choose has become the heart that can only resist.

— *PNEUMA*
